evidence: ConnectionMethod is defined three times as identical string unions: packages/shared/types/agent.ts (ConnectionMethod), packages/web/lib/agent-factory.ts (ConnectionMethodValue), and packages/web/components/modals/agent/types.ts (ConnectionMethodType) — no single source of truth at the boundary

Agent API key verification logic (hash + Prisma lookup + isActive check + result mapping) is implemented in three independent locations: agent-auth.ts, route-handlers.js::authenticateAgentKey/createAgentVerifyHandler, and api/internal/agents/verify/route.ts — each with slightly different return shapes and error semantics

agent-auth.ts contains two functions (authenticateAgentRequest and authenticateAgentKey) that are near-identical: same Prisma query, same select shape, same result mapping — differing only in how the API key is extracted (from header vs direct string)

MessageMetadata receives metadata as Record<string, unknown> and immediately casts six fields with 'as' assertions — the boundary contract is untyped despite the metadata shape being well-known from the streaming protocol
ChatArea cleanly composes ChannelHeader, MessageList, MessageInput, and TypingIndicator with explicit props — handoff is minimal and predictable
admin-auth.ts and internal-auth.ts each have focused, single-purpose contracts with clear security properties — good boundary design
agent-factory.ts properly centralizes agent creation logic used by two different route handlers (UI and CLI bootstrap) with explicit options/result interfaces

strengths: ChatArea composes child components (ChannelHeader, MessageList, MessageInput, TypingIndicator) with explicit, minimal prop interfaces — a developer can understand the composition at a glance
agent-factory.ts serves as a proper shared creation boundary used by both UI and CLI bootstrap routes, with explicit CreateAgentOptions/CreateAgentResult interfaces
admin-auth.ts and internal-auth.ts are well-scoped boundary modules with single-purpose contracts, consistent return types, and documented security properties
useChannel hook encapsulates all Phoenix Channel complexity behind a clean UseChannelReturn interface — callers never touch socket state directly
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: The primary deficiency is duplication at integration seams: the same domain concept (connection method type, agent key verification) is defined and implemented independently in 2-3 places with subtly different interfaces, creating glue-code entropy and drift risk at the agent auth and type boundaries.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The frontend composition layer shows solid mid-level design — ChatArea, ChannelHeader, and the useChannel hook hand off data through clean, explicit interfaces. However, the agent authentication and type boundaries are significantly duplicated: agent key verification is implemented three times with different return shapes, and connection method types are defined three times as independent string unions. This duplication means a developer making cross-module changes to agent auth or connection methods must discover and update multiple divergent implementations. The mix of clean frontend seams and duplicated backend seams places this in the 70-75 range — enough inconsistency that a developer cannot fully trust patterns seen in one area when working in another.

issues.summary: ConnectionMethod string union defined independently in three files with no shared source of truth
evidence: packages/shared/types/agent.ts defines ConnectionMethod as a union of 6 string literals
packages/web/lib/agent-factory.ts defines VALID_CONNECTION_METHODS array and derives ConnectionMethodValue — same 6 values
packages/web/components/modals/agent/types.ts defines ConnectionMethodType — same 6 values
No import relationship exists between these three definitions; adding a new method requires updating all three independently
issues.suggestion: Use the shared type from packages/shared/types/agent.ts as the single source of truth. In agent-factory.ts, import ConnectionMethod and derive the runtime array from it. In modals/agent/types.ts, import and re-export ConnectionMethod instead of redefining it.

issues.summary: Agent API key hash-and-lookup logic implemented in three separate locations with divergent return shapes
evidence: agent-auth.ts::authenticateAgentRequest performs SHA-256 hash, findFirst on agentRegistration, checks isActive, returns AgentAuthResult
route-handlers.js::authenticateAgentKey performs same hash and findFirst but returns {authorized, error, status} — different shape
route-handlers.js::createAgentVerifyHandler duplicates the same hash+findFirst+isActive logic again for the internal verify endpoint
api/internal/agents/verify/route.ts has yet another independent implementation of the same hash+findFirst+isActive flow
Each implementation has slightly different error responses and Prisma select shapes for the same underlying operation
issues.suggestion: Extract a single shared function (e.g., verifyAgentApiKey in agent-auth.ts) that takes an API key string, performs the hash+lookup+isActive check, and returns a discriminated union result. Have all three call sites (agent-auth, route-handlers, internal verify route) use this shared function and translate the result to their specific response format.

issues.summary: authenticateAgentRequest and authenticateAgentKey in agent-auth.ts duplicate the same Prisma query and mapping
evidence: authenticateAgentRequest (line 27-68) and authenticateAgentKey (line 74-113) share identical Prisma query structure (same where clause, same select shape, same isActive check, same result mapping)
The only difference is header extraction vs direct string parameter — the core verification logic is copy-pasted
issues.suggestion: Have authenticateAgentRequest extract the key from the header and delegate to authenticateAgentKey for the actual verification, eliminating the duplicated Prisma query and result mapping.

issues.summary: MessageMetadata receives Record<string, unknown> and uses 6 type assertions instead of a typed interface
evidence: MessageMetadata accepts metadata: Record<string, unknown> (line 6-7)
Lines 17-22 immediately cast model, provider, tokensIn, tokensOut, latencyMs, costUsd with 'as' assertions
The metadata shape is well-known from the streaming protocol (TASK-0039) and use-channel.ts already types it as Record<string, unknown> | null in MessagePayload
This means the boundary between the streaming protocol and the UI has no type enforcement — metadata shape changes would not produce compile errors
issues.suggestion: Define an AgentMessageMetadata interface (e.g., in packages/shared/types or alongside MessageMetadata) with the known fields (model, provider, tokensIn, tokensOut, latencyMs, costUsd) as optional typed properties. Use this interface as the prop type and in MessagePayload, replacing the Record<string, unknown> at the boundary.

root_causes: Agent authentication and connection method types grew organically across internal API routes, public V1 routes, and UI components without establishing a single shared boundary module — each integration point re-implemented rather than composed
The route-handlers.js file predates the TypeScript agent-auth.ts module and was never consolidated, leaving two parallel auth implementations
likely_symptoms: near_identical_agent_auth_functions is a symptom of triplicated_agent_key_verification — fixing the root shared function would naturally collapse the internal duplication
